coredata.dc:title: A sandwich−type photoelectrochemical sensor constructed with a signal amplification strategy based on the upconversion luminescence characteristics of Ag@N−CQDs for sensitive detection of neuron specific enolase
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hanyu
Li, Yuyang
Gao, Zhongfeng
Ma, Hongmin
Ren, Xiang
Fan, Dawei
Wang, Huan
Wei, Qin
Wu, Dan
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composite nanomaterials and PDA@Ag@N−CQDs were synthesized, and used as substrates and second antibody label respectively to construct a sandwich photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor. The upconversion luminescence effect of N−CQDs can convert long wavelength light into short wavelength light that can be utilized by the substrate material, which can provide additional excitation light energy for the substrate material and further enhance the photoelectric response. Besides, Ag has SPR effect and can also promote electron transfer. The proposed sandwich immunosensor achieves detection of NSE in the concentration range of 0.001 ng mL−1 to 100 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.28 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). What's more, the proposed sensor also exhibits good stability, selectivity, as well as reproducibility, indicating its promising application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion luminescence effect
PEC sensor
PDA@Ag@N−CQDs
Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible electron transfer pathway of sandwich PEC immunosensor
Exploration of the optimal experimental condition
Study on the performance of PEC immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) X−ray diffraction patterns of Bi2S3 and AgBiS2. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of synthesized materials, (B) Bi2S3, (C) AgBiS2, (D) Bi2S3/AgBiS2 and (E) PDA. (F) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of Ag@N−CQDs.
(A) Element mapping of Bi2S3/AgBiS2 nanocomposite and the distribution of element Bi (B), Ag (C) and S (D).

EIS (A) and photocurrent response (B): (a) ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2, (b) ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1, (c) ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1/BSA, (d) ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1/BSA/NSE and (e) ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1/BSA/NSE/PDA@Ag@N−CQDs@Ab2.

Photocurrent intensity under different experimental conditions: (A) recombination time of Bi2S3/AgBiS2, (B) molar ratio of AgBiS2 to Bi2S3, (C) bias voltage (D) concentration of AA, (E) pH of PBS and (F) incubation time (c NSE = 0.1 ng mL−1).

(A) Photocurrent intensity under varying NSE concentrations, a–i: the concentration range of NSE is from 0.001 to 100 ng mL−1 (0.001 ng mL−1, 0.005 ng mL−1, 0.02 ng mL−1, 0.1 ng mL−1, 0.5 ng mL−1, 2.0 ng mL−1, 5.0 ng mL−1, 20 ng mL−1 and 100 ng mL−1); (B) linear calibration curve, measured in 0.1 mol L−1 AA PBS (pH = 7.4) buffer solution, with bias of 0 V; (C) specificity of PEC sensor, including: (a) 0.1 ng mL−1 NSE, (b) 0.1 ng mL−1 NSE+10 ng mL−1 CEA, (c) 0.1 ng mL−1 NSE+10 ng mL−1 PSA, (d) 0.1 ng mL−1 NSE+10 ng mL−1 glutamate, (e) 0.1 ng mL−1 NSE+10 ng mL−1 CYFRA21−1; (D) photocurrent intensity of on−off lamp for continuous 200 s (c NSE = 2.0 ng mL−1 (purple line) and 0.001 ng mL−1 (blue line), the ruler is 10 μA).
Construction process of the PEC immunosensor.
Possible electron transfer pathway of sandwich PEC immunosensor based on Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composite.

head.ce:title: A sandwich−type photoelectrochemical sensor constructed with a signal amplification strategy based on the upconversion luminescence characteristics of Ag@N−CQDs for sensitive detection of neuron specific enolase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composite nanomaterials and PDA@Ag@N−CQDs were synthesized, and used as substrates and second antibody label respectively to construct a sandwich photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor. The upconversion luminescence effect of N−CQDs can convert long wavelength light into short wavelength light that can be utilized by the substrate material, which can provide additional excitation light energy for the substrate material and further enhance the photoelectric response. Besides, Ag has SPR effect and can also promote electron transfer. The proposed sandwich immunosensor achieves detection of NSE in the concentration range of 0.001 ng mL−1 to 100 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.28 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). What's more, the proposed sensor also exhibits good stability, selectivity, as well as reproducibility, indicating its promising application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Small cell lung cancer (SCLC), as a common malignant tumor worldwide, has the characteristics of fast metastasis and high mortality rate [1,2]. Generally speaking, the infection of SCLC is closely related to smoking, and patients often experience metastatic diseases [3]. Neuron specific enolase (NSE), an emerging tumor marker, has significant value in the early diagnosis and clinical analysis of SCLC due to its high specificity [4–6]. From the perspective of human health and gradually overcoming SCLC, achieving sensitive and rapid detection of NSE is particularly crucial. Up to now, many methods have been reported to detect NSE, such as surface−enhanced Raman scattering [7], fluorometric immunoassay [8], electrochemistry [9,10] and electrochemiluminescence [11,12]. In recent years, PEC sensor, a type of electrochemical sensing, has developed rapidly, achieving the conversion of optical signal to electrical signal [13,14]. Due to its good stability, miniaturization, strong portability, wide linear range, and fast analysis speed, PEC sensor has been widely used in the detection of biomarkers, environmental pollutants and food contaminants [15–21]. For example, Chen et al. constructed a PEC sensing platform for ultra-sensitive near-infrared response detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 by assembling flexible conductive paper electrodes and UCNP@SiO2@Ag/C-g-C3N4 [22]. This method provides a new perspective for developing diversified and portable PEC detection devices.

As is well known, the selection of the substrate material plays an important role in the construction process of PEC sensor. Sulfide semiconductors have higher photoelectric conversion efficiency due to their high light absorption coefficient and low free carrier absorption, which makes them widely used in PEC sensors [23]. Bismuth sulfide (Bi2S3), as a kind of n−type direct band gap material (∼1.28 eV), can effectively absorb visible light and has very high photoelectric conversion efficiency, which makes it a photoelectric active material favored by researchers [24–28]. However, under visible light irradiation, the electrons on the valence band of Bi2S3 transition to the conduction band, producing electron hole pairs. Due to the instability of electrons in the conduction band, they are easily returned to the valence band, resulting in higher recombination rates of photo generated electrons and holes [29,30]. In view of this phenomenon, it is often used to compound with other substances to improve performance. As a ternary chalcogenide compound, AgBiS2 shows a commendable band gap structure coupled with a pronounced light absorption coefficient (∼1.20 eV), making it extensively used in various aspects [31,32]. Therefore, the PEC performance of Bi2S3 is improved by combining with AgBiS2 in this paper.

In order to further realize signal amplification, the selection of secondary antibody label is also particularly essential. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit various advantages, such as chemical stability, upconversion luminescence behavior, and unique charge transfer properties [33,34]. Nevertheless, the application of CQDs is still limited due to its low photosensitive efficiency. In addition, the introduction of nitrogen into CQDs can effectively induce charge delocalization, thereby promoting the charge transfer ability of CQDs [35–37]. Metals with surface plasmon resonance (SPR), such as Au and Ag, have been introduced into photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical analysis systems, mainly because plasma metals can improve both light absorption and charge separation efficiency [38,39]. Inspired by these properties, PDA@Ag@N−CQDs as a secondary antibody label was introduced to PEC sensing, hoping to achieve signal amplification effect.

In this manuscript, a sandwich−type photoelectrochemical immunosensor for detecting NSE was successfully constructed (Scheme 1 ). Simply put, the Bi2S3/AgBiS2 nanocomposite was successfully dropped onto the ITO electrode and an obvious photocurrent intensity was obtained. Ag@N−CQDs can not only serve as electronic media to promote charge transfer, but also become photosensitizers due to the strong affinity between Ag and N−CQDs [40]. Finally, NSE was selected as the target to verify the practicability.
All chemicals, reagents, apparatus used in the experiments and the synthesis of Bi2S3 and AgBiS2 are presented in the Supplementary materials.

The synthesized AgBiS2 and Bi2S3 (molar ratio is 0.2) were introduced into a beaker holding 20 mL of ultrapure water. Following strong stirring for 30 min, the resultant solution was transferred to an autoclave, where it underwent a reaction at 120 °C for a period of 1 h. Subsequently, the product was subjected to three cycles of centrifugal washing, employing absolute ethanol and ultrapure water.

N−CQDs were synthesized according to relevant literature report [41]. 5 mmol ammonium citrate was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water (containing 335 μL ethylenediamine), and continuously stirred for 10 min to make it evenly mixed. The above solution was then transferred to a 25 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, yellow N−CQDs were dissolved in ultrapure water for 24 h to obtain bright yellow N−CQDs solution, which was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for standby.

0.5 g AgNO3 was ultrasonic dispersed in 50 mL N−CQDs prepared. Then, the dispersed solution was immediately transferred and reacted in a water bath at 55 °C for 5 min. Finally, Ag@N−CQDs were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C.

Polydopamine (PDA) was synthesized by oxidative polymerization. Specifically, 100 mg dopamine hydrochloride was dissolved in 100 mL 0.01 mol L−1 Tris−HCl solution (pH = 8.8) at first, and then 50 mL isopropanol was added. After the mixed solution was stirred in the dark for 24 h, the obtained product was rinsed three times with ultrapure water.

500 μL PDA was diluted to 1.5 mL ultrapure water and recorded as solution A. In addition, add 50 μL EDC/NHS in 500 μL Ag@N−CQDs and shake at 4 °C for 2 h, record as solution B. Then, solution A was added to solution B to mix. After 6 h of shaking, 1 mL Ab2 (2 μg mL−1) was added to the above mixed solution. After further shaking, the obtained solution was centrifuged, and the final product was dispersed in 1 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) for storage.

Initially, 10 μL of the Bi2S3/AgBiS2 nanocomposite was applied onto ITO conductive glass using a pipette gun. The coated substrate was dried at room temperature for a duration of 1 h. Subsequently, 3 μL of 3 mmol L−1 solution of thioglycolic acid was dispensed onto the ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2 electrode. Following a drying period of 30 min, 2 μL of EDC/NHS was introduced dropwise and left to air−dry at room temperature for an additional 30 min. Subsequent to this, an additional 5 μL of 1 μg mL−1 solution of Ab1 was applied, and the ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1 electrode was subjected to an incubation period in a 4 °C refrigerator lasting 30 min. Upon the addition of 3 μL of 0.1 % BSA solution, the ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1/BSA electrode was incubated for 30 min. Subsequently, 5 μL of NSE with varying concentrations were applied onto the aforementioned electrode and incubated for 1 h. Finally, 3 μL of 10 μg mL−1 PDA@Ag@N−CQDs@Ab2 was dropped on the surface of the electrode, and the PEC immunosensor was constructed.

The measurement of photocurrent intensity was conducted employing an electrochemical workstation and a chronoamperometric i−t curve methodology. The electrolyte utilized was 10 mL PBS solution containing 0.1 mol L−1 of AA (pH = 7.4). The prepared ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2/Ab1/BSA/NSE/PDA@Ag@N−CQDs@Ab2 electrode served as the working electrode (WE). Additionally, a saturated calomel electrode and a Pt electrode were employed as the reference electrode (RE) and the counter electrode (CE) respectively. Notably, no bias is applied during the entire process.

Fig. 1 A illustrates the XRD patterns of the composite materials. For AgBiS2, distinct characteristic peaks of AgBiS2 appear at positions of 27.5°, 31.7°, 45.8°, 54.2°, 56.4°, and 66.2°, corresponding to the crystal forms of (111), (200), (220), (311), (222) and (400), respectively, which confirms the successful synthesis of AgBiS2 (PDF#21−1178) [42]. The characteristic diffraction peaks of Bi2S3 appear at 15.8°, 17.6°, 22.4°, 23.7°, 24.9°, 28.6°, 31.8°, 32.9°, 33.9°, 35.6°, 39.9°, 42.7°, 45.5°, 46.5°, 49.1°, 52.6°, 59.1°, 62.7°, 65.2° and 72.0°, which is corresponding to the lattice planes of (020), (120), (220), (101), (130), (211), (221), (301), (311), (240), (141), (421), (002), (431), (251), (351), (242), (152), (721) and (741). The aforementioned results are consistent with the data on standard card (PDF#17−0320), validating the successful synthesis of Bi2S3 [43].

The SEM analysis of Bi2S3 is illustrated in Fig. 1B. Evidently, Bi2S3 exhibits a consistently distributed rod−shaped architecture, boasting a length spanning approximately 100–200 nm. The tight binding of these rod−shaped substances also provides more possibilities for their binding with other substances. As depicted in Fig. 1B, for AgBiS2, a wide distribution of nanoparticles resembling coral shapes was observed at a magnification of 100 nm. Upon the combination of AgBiS2 and Bi2S3, the morphology of the composite material is shown in Fig. 1D. A large number of rod−shaped and coral shaped nanoparticles were observed to accumulate together, which to some extent confirms the successful recombination of AgBiS2 and Bi2S3. The SEM image (Fig. 1E) shows the morphology of PDA produced by the oxidative polymerization of dopamine hydrochloride. Obviously, under high−power SEM observation, the sample exhibits a regular spherical shape. The TEM image of Ag@N−CQDs is shown in Fig. 1F, and it can be clearly observed that black dots with a size of several nanometers.

In order to further verify the successful synthesis of Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composite materials, elemental mapping was characterized (Fig. 2 A). Within the same observation range, elements Bi, Ag, and S all exhibit uniform and large distributions, further confirming the success of composite material preparation, which is also an effective supplement to the other characterization methods mentioned above (Fig. 2B−D).
The inset in Fig. 3 A shows the EIS equivalent circuit.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a key method for describing electrode interface characteristics and evaluating the construction process of PEC sensors. Fig. 3A shows the EIS spectrum measured after different modifications of ITO electrode. The tested solutions were 0.1 mol L−1 KCl and 2.5 mmol·L−1 Fe(CN)63−/4−. The impedance magnitude on the electrode surface exhibits a progressive augmentation concomitant with the modification of diverse substances layer by layer. This phenomenon arises from the impeding effect on electron transfer at the electrode surface induced by the affixed material. The equivalent current diagram inserted in Fig. 3A was simulated by using ZSimpWin software, including solution resistance (R s), electron transfer resistance (R et), double layer capacitance (C dl) and Warburg impedance (Z w). The results are shown in Table S1(Supplementary material). The R et value in the table can also reflect that the impedance value gradually increases during the process of layer by layer modification. Fig. 3B demonstrates i−t diagrams of the working electrode and the photocurrent was measured in a 0.1 mol L−1 PBS solution (pH = 7.4, containing in 0.1 mol L−1). Owing to the excellent photoelectric conversion performance of the Bi2S3/AgBiS2 composite, the photocurrent exhibited by the ITO/Bi2S3/AgBiS2 electrode (curve b) is markedly heightened in comparison to that of the bare electrode (curve a). Through the stepwise modification of Ab1, BSA and NSE, the photocurrent intensity (represented by curves c ∼ e) experiences a gradual diminution. Primarily, this decline ensues from the layer−by−layer modification of the electrode, thereby increasing the transfer resistance of interfacial electrons. When PDA@Ag@N−CQDs@Ab2 was modified on the electrode, the photocurrent signal increased slightly due to the plasmon resonance effect of Ag nanoparticles and the upconversion luminescence characteristics of N−CQDs. In addition, the photocurrent of the sensor is compared when PDA@Ag@N−CQDs and Ag@N−CQDs are used as secondary antibody labels respectively, and the results show that the effect of PDA is better (Fig. S1). Fig. 3A and B together verify that the designed sensor was successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible electron transfer pathway of sandwich PEC immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The bandgap energies of Bi2S3 and AgBiS2 were calculated to be 1.28 eV and 1.20 eV, respectively (Figs. S2A and S2C). Besides, by measuring the Mott-Schottky curve, the valence band potential (E CB) values of Bi2S3 and AgBiS2 were obtained to be −0.45 V and −0.62 V, respectively (Figs. S2B and S2D). Based on the conduction band and bandgap, the valence band potential (E VB) of Bi2S3 and AgBiS2 can be calculated as 0.83 V and 0.58 V, respectively. The stepped band edge energy levels between Bi2S3 and AgBiS2 conform to energy level matching, which is more conducive to improving the separation and transfer efficiency of photo generated carriers.

As depicted in Scheme 2 , when the substrate material is illuminated, electrons on the conduction band of AgBiS2 are transferred to the conduction band of Bi2S3 and ultimately reach the ITO substrate. In order to avoid the excessive accumulation of photogenerated holes in the valence band affecting the transfer of electrons, ascorbic acid solution was used to consume the photogenerated holes, so as to obtain a stable photocurrent. Moreover, to optimize the performance of the sensor, PDA@Ag@N−CQDs as a secondary antibody label can further enhance the photocurrent. On the one hand, the upconversion luminescence of N−CQDs can absorb long wavelength light and convert it into visible light, which is provided to the substrate material as an additional light source. On the other hand, the electrons generated by the SPR effect of Ag nanoparticles are also transferred to the substrate material, and finally to the electrode. Besides, the experiment on the SPR effect of Ag was shown in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the optimal experimental condition
ce:section.ce:para: To maximize the performance of the sensor, the recombination time of the substrate Bi2S3/AgBiS2, the amount of substance ratio, and the bias voltage of the sensor were optimized. Fig. 4 A shows the effect of the recombination time of Bi2S3/AgBiS2 on the photocurrent. From the experimental results, it can be seen that the photocurrent response gradually increases with the increase of the recombination reaction time, and the intensity reaches the peak at about 30 min. After that the recombination reaction time increases and the photocurrent decreases. Fig. 4B manifests the influence molar ratio of AgBiS2 to Bi2S3 on the photocurrent intensity. When the molar ratio is about 0.2, the photocurrent response reaches the maximum value. Fig. 4C illustrates the effect of the operating bias on the photocurrent response. From the experimental results, the photocurrent intensity reaches a peak at a bias of 0 V. Additionally, the electrolyte concentration has the potential to influence the photocurrent intensity of the PEC sensor. As shown in Fig. 4D, photocurrent signal reaches the maximum with a concentration of 0.1 mol L−1. Hence, 0.1 mol L−1 AA solution was selected for the subsequent experiments. The pH value has an impact on the activity of biomolecules, therefore the photocurrent intensity under different pH was investigated (Fig. 4E). The result reveals that the peak photocurrent is obtained at pH = 7.4, as the neutral environment proves most propitious for the viability of protein components on the electrode. Fig. 4F shows the effect of incubation time on photocurrent response after the addition of NSE. As the incubation time was extended from 0.25 to 1 h, the photocurrent showed a continuous rise and eventually reached a plateau, indicating that the introduced antigen and antibody were fully bound. Therefore, 1 h was selected as the best incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the performance of PEC immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental parameters, the sensor was employed for the detection of NSE at various concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the photocurrent intensity indicates a significant enhancement with the increase of NSE concentration. Besides, Fig. 5B exhibits that the photocurrent of the sensor with a concentration range of 0.001 ng mL−1∼100 ng mL−1 has a good linear relationship with the logarithm of NSE concentration. The obtained linear equation is I (μA) = 32.06 + 4.865 lg c (ng·mL−1), the square of the correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.991, and the detection limit is 0.28 pg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Compared with other relevant literature on detecting NSE, the PEC sensor proposed in this paper exhibits satisfactory performance (Table S2, supplementary materials).

Selectivity is one of the important factors in evaluating the excellent performance of sensors. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), prostate specific antigen (PSA), glutamate and CYFRA21−1 were selected as interference substances to test the specificity of the sensor prepared. The results were shown in Fig. 5C. The photocurrent measured in the presence of excessive interference substances does not change significantly compared with that without interference substances, indicating that the sensor has good selectivity. Moreover, the stability of the sensor was evaluated by measuring the intensity of the photocurrent for 200 s at different concentrations of NSE. The small change in the photocurrent shown in Fig. 5D can indicate that the PEC sensor has satisfactory stability.

The feasibility of the sensor was investigated by standard addition method. Before measurement, the initial sample was centrifuged twice to obtain a supernatant (9000 rpm). NSE standard solutions of 2.00 ng mL−1, 5.00 ng mL−1 and 10.0 ng mL−1 were added to the initial sample and determined. The results are shown in Table S3. Obviously, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is 1.5 %–2.9 %, and the recovery rate is 98.8 %–102 %, indicating that the sensor has a good application prospect in practical NSE detection.

In this paper, Bi2S3/AgBiS2 nanocomposites as the matrix materials and PDA@Ag@N−CQDs as the secondary antibody label, which facilitated the construction of a sandwich−type PEC immunosensor for the detection of NSE. The sensor exhibited a linear range from 0.001 ng mL−1 to 100 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.28 pg mL−1. Furthermore, the anti−interference capability and stability of the sensor were investigated, yielding satisfactory results. Finally, the practicality was validated through the analysis and detection of samples, confirming its auspicious potential for application in the analysis and detection of NSE.

Hanyu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Yuyang Li: Data curation. Zhongfeng Gao: Data curation. Hongmin Ma: Methodology, Data curation. Xiang Ren: Formal analysis, Data curation. Dawei Fan: Formal analysis. Huan Wang: Methodology. Qin Wei: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Dan Wu: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274063) and the Young Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province (tsqn201909124). All the authors express their sincere thanks.
